vulnerabilities.description: User-supplied credentials are serialized (PHP serialize format) and base64-encoded into the 'creds' cookie, which is later parsed server-side to render the username. The cookie is client-controlled and unsigned, indicating reliance on unserialize() of attacker input and potential for object injection or logic manipulation.
Identity is derived from a client-controlled 'creds' cookie. Modifying the cookie changes the displayed user, suggesting potential impersonation if server-side checks are weak or inconsistent.
The 'creds' cookie is issued without HttpOnly or Secure attributes and contains credentials in base64, making it susceptible to theft and tampering.
Server headers reveal Apache/2.4.25 (Debian) and X-Powered-By: PHP/5.6.40, which are outdated and may have known vulnerabilities.
Login form lacks anti-CSRF tokens, allowing cross-site request forgery to set or alter the 'creds' cookie.
Responses lack standard hardening headers such as Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, and X-Content-Type-Options.
